data.title: Nvidia and TSMC complete first made-in-the-USA Blackwell chip wafer: report
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Taiwan Semiconductor Company (NYSE:TSM) have completed their first made-in-the-USA wafer, which will be used for the eventual production of Blackwell chips, according to Axios.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang is expected to make the announcement from TSMC's manufacturing facility in Phoenix, Ariz., sometime today, the report said.

"Nvidia and TSMC are working together to build the infrastructure that powers the world's AI factories, right here in America," Nvidia said, according to the report.

It appears the efforts of U.S. President Donald Trump to manufacture more high tech domestically, as well as the CHIPS and Science Act, which was signed into law by former U.S. President Joe Biden in 2022, are starting to produce results.

"TSMC Arizona is expected to create thousands of high-tech jobs and attract a broad ecosystem of suppliers," both companies said in a joint statement.

data.title: CEO of Liberty Energy, founded by Trump’s energy secretary, slams 'punitive tariff policies'
data.description:
Liberty Energy (NYSE:LBRT) closed +28.3% on Friday to a six-month high despite reporting adjusted earnings and revenues that missed estimates, as positive commentary regarding its FY 2026-27 outlook outweighed Q3 results.

Liberty (NYSE:LBRT) executives noted on the company's earnings conference call the rapid expansion of its power generation business, which supplies electricity services to oil drillers and data center developers, expecting to add 500 MW of generation by the end of FY 2026 and build total capacity to more than 1 GW by the end of 2027, as reported by Bloomberg.

CEO Ron Gusek, who took over the leadership role at Liberty (NYSE:LBRT) earlier this year after predecessor and company founder Chris Wright departed to lead the U.S. Department of Energy, criticized "the current punitive tariff policies" during the call.

Tariffs on steel and other oilfield staples are bad for business, hurt competitiveness, and will hinder the Trump administration's stated goal of dominating the artificial intelligence sector, Gusek said.

"They're a path to mediocrity, not excellence," Gusek said, according to Bloomberg. "They raise prices, cut profits, increase unemployment, diminish productivity and slow economic growth.'

data.title: Sonder working with Kirkland & Ellis on a potential restructuring - report
data.description:
• Sonder Holdings (NASDAQ:SOND) is working with Kirkland & Ellis as a legal adviser on a potential restructuring of the hospitality company, according to a posting on X from an Octus Credit (formerly Reorg) reporter. 
  • Last month, Sonder announced that management and the board of directors were evaluating financing and other strategic alternatives in consultation with its financial advisor Moelis & Co. and outside counsel. The company also announced at the same time that its chief real estate officer was resigning in connection with possible plans to participate in a potential bid for the company. 
  • Shares of Sonder (NASDAQ:SOND) fell 14% on Friday.
  • Sonder didn't immediately return Seeking Alpha's email request for comment.
  • Sonder reported its Q2 results on Tuesday.

data.title: Fifth Third outlines merger-driven growth and expects full year adjusted PPNR to rise 7%-8% while pausing share repurchases
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Timothy Spence highlighted the announced merger between Fifth Third and Comerica, stating, "Our M&A framework has been consistent. First, that M&A is not a strategy unto itself, but rather a means to achieve stated strategic objectives. Second, that the cash earn back, IRR and NPV of synergies must be superior to organic alternatives to justify higher execution risk. And third, that the outcome must be a company that is better and not just bigger. We believe this is one of those rare combinations that satisfies all 3 criteria." Spence emphasized that the merger should create a "well-diversified, even more profitable company with even better long-term growth."
  • Spence noted that Q3 earnings per share were $0.91, or $0.93 excluding certain items, and that results included a nearly $200 million provision expense linked to fraud at Tricolor. Average loans increased 6% year-over-year and average demand deposits rose 3%, with consumer DDA growth at 6%. Adjusted revenues were up 6%, driven by a 7% improvement in net interest income and 5% fee growth, while adjusted PPNR increased 11% and operating leverage was 330 basis points positive.
  • Spence reported tangible book value per share grew 7% year-over-year and 3% sequentially. The company repurchased $300 million of stock and increased its common dividend by 8% during the quarter. He cited Southeast expansion, including 13 new branches in the quarter and plans for 27 more by year-end, with consumer households in the Southeast up 7% year-over-year.
  • Spence stated, "We will deliver both" record NII and 150 to 200 basis points of positive operating leverage for the full year. Looking ahead, he expressed excitement about the Comerica merger and ongoing investments, including 60 new branches planned for next year.
  • CFO Bryan Preston said, "Third quarter results reflect disciplined execution of our strategic priorities. Expanding in the Southeast, scaling payments in Newline and maintaining operational efficiency while delivering strong performance in a rapidly changing environment. Adjusted revenue was $2.3 billion, our highest since 2022."

Outlook

  • Preston outlined expectations for Q4, stating, "We expect NII to be stable to up 1% from the third quarter due to loan and core deposit growth. This outlook assumes two 25 basis point rate cuts during the fourth quarter."
  • Average total loan balances are expected to be up 1%, with adjusted noninterest income up 2% to 3% and adjusted noninterest expense up 2%. He added, "Our guide implies full year adjusted revenue to be up nearly 5% and PPNR to grow 7% to 8%."
  • Preston expects Q4 net charge-offs around 40 basis points. The company will pause share repurchases until the Comerica acquisition closes, anticipated by the end of Q1 2026.

Financial Results

  • Spence reported Q3 earnings per share of $0.91, or $0.93 excluding certain items. Adjusted revenues rose 6%, net interest income grew 7% year-over-year and 2% sequentially, and net interest margin expanded for the seventh straight quarter.
  • Average loans increased 6% year-over-year, and average noninterest-bearing deposits grew 1% sequentially and 3% year-over-year. Adjusted noninterest income grew 7% sequentially and 5% year-over-year. Wealth fees rose 11%, and capital markets fees were up 28% sequentially and 4% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted noninterest expense increased 3% year-over-year and 2% sequentially. Headcount was down 1% year-over-year, with value stream programs expected to generate $200 million in annual run-rate savings by year-end.
  • Net charge-off ratio was 109 basis points including $178 million in net charge-offs from Tricolor. Provision expense included a $142 million reduction in allowance for credit losses. CET1 ratio ended at 10.54%.

Q&A

  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC Capital Markets, asked about the Comerica transaction reception. Spence responded, "It's just been, I think, positive all the way across the Board... All the early engagement that we have done with the regulators has been constructive."
  • Cassidy inquired about contagion risk in the NDFI portfolio. Chief Credit Officer Greg Schroeck explained, "We have maintained low levels. We're the lowest -- one of the lowest levels of NDFI concentrations of large banks. We're at about 8% of the total portfolio." He detailed diversification and confidence in portfolio quality post comprehensive review.
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities, questioned commercial client sentiment. Spence observed, "The close of the quarter, I was out in several of our markets...the quarter-to-quarter went to the client, we referred to as outlook 'nauseously optimistic.' The tariff uncertainty absolutely continues to weigh on any clients that are exposed."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley, asked about Direct Express and credit guidance. Preston said, "On average, it's about $3.5 billion of DDA that will obviously provide a lot of funding benefits associated with our balance sheet," and described a 15%-20% margin on associated fees. Schroeck stated, "I still expect full year charge-offs to land in the midpoint of our original guidance range...I continue to be very confident that the commercial loss rates return to that mid 30, 35 basis point range in the fourth quarter."
  • Erika Najarian, UBS, asked about funding strategy. Spence responded, "The priority here is replacing the funding and then supporting the right level of growth beyond that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about merger integration, NDFI risk, commercial client sentiment, and deposit funding, with a tone that was largely neutral but at times slightly cautious regarding risk management and integration.
  • Management responses were confident and detailed, emphasizing discipline and strong execution, particularly regarding the Comerica merger, credit quality, and funding strategy. Spence stated, "We are feeling very good about our ability to get the outcomes."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained engaged but the tone shifted to address the Comerica merger and Tricolor fraud, while management maintained a confident and constructive stance, especially about strategic execution and risk controls.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter included the major announcement of the Comerica merger, which was not present in the previous quarter. Strategic focus has shifted to integration, synergy realization, and expansion in Texas.
  • Management continues to prioritize organic growth and Southeast expansion but now emphasizes merger-driven scale and diversification. Operating leverage and profitability guidance have improved from the prior quarter.
  • Analysts shifted from general questions on growth and credit to more detailed inquiries on merger integration, NDFI exposure, and risk management.
  • Key metrics such as EPS, loan growth, and operating leverage remain strong, but the quarter was impacted by the Tricolor fraud provision, resulting in higher charge-offs and provision expense.
  • Management confidence remains high, with more detailed discussion of integration plans and synergies versus the last call.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the $200 million provision expense due to Tricolor fraud and detailed a comprehensive review of the NDFI portfolio, emphasizing low concentrations and strong underwriting.
  • Schroeck described enhancements to processes and continued monitoring of risk, particularly in the asset-backed finance portfolio, and highlighted the engagement of a third party to validate collateral.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about merger execution, integration risks, and potential funding challenges, which management addressed with updated guidance and operational detail.

Final Takeaway

Fifth Third Bancorp is entering a new phase with the Comerica merger, emphasizing operational discipline, scale, and synergy realization, while maintaining strong profitability metrics and expanding its Southeast and middle market footprint. Management remains confident in achieving record NII, positive operating leverage, and strong returns, even as it pauses share repurchases to support the upcoming closing of the acquisition. The company continues to focus on risk management and integration readiness, positioning itself for enhanced growth and profitability in 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amgen, AstraZeneca gets FDA nod for Tezspire
data.description:
• Amgen (NASDAQ:AMGN) and AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) said that the FDA has approved Tezspire as a maintenance treatment for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps in patients aged 12 and older.
  • This FDA approval is supported by data demonstrating the drug's efficacy and safety from the WAYPOINT Phase III clinical trial.
  • Additionally, applications for regulatory approval are currently being evaluated in Europe, China, Japan, and various other nations.

data.title: Regions Financial expects full-year 2025 net interest income growth of 3%–4% while advancing technology modernization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Regions Financial Corporation (RF) Q3 2025

Management View

  • John Turner, President, CEO & Chairman, opened the call by highlighting strong quarterly earnings of $548 million, resulting in earnings per share of $0.61. On an adjusted basis, earnings were $561 million or $0.63 per share. Turner stated, "We delivered adjusted pretax pre-provision income of $830 million, a 4% increase year-over-year, and we generated a strong return on tangible common equity of 19%." He noted continued top-quartile deposit growth, above-peer median change in market share, and the lowest deposit cost among peers. The company grew total average deposits and accounts across consumer checking, small business, and wealth management. Loan commitments increased by approximately $2 billion year-to-date, and pipelines are almost doubled year-over-year. Turner emphasized ongoing portfolio shaping in higher-risk leveraged lending segments and a meaningful increase in loans refinanced off-balance sheet through debt capital markets. Turner also detailed progress on core technology modernization, with a commercial loan system upgrade to a cloud platform in the first half of 2026 and pilots for a cloud-based deposit system beginning late 2026, with full conversion anticipated in 2027.

  • David Turner, Senior EVP & CFO, reported, "Average loans grew 1%, while ending declined 1%." He noted continued portfolio shaping, with $900 million in targeted loans exited year-to-date and another $300 million to address by year-end. Turner noted, "Deposits remained strong overall," and said, "We now expect average deposits to be up low single digits versus the prior year." Regarding net interest income, he stated, "Net interest income was relatively stable linked quarter... net interest income grew modestly, benefiting primarily from new fixed-rate asset originations and reinvestments in today's elevated rate environment." On noninterest income, Turner said, "We see continued opportunity to grow clients through both new relationships and within our existing customer base."

Outlook

  • The company now expects full-year 2025 average loans to remain relatively stable versus 2024. Management increased expectations for full-year average deposit balances, now expecting average deposits to be up low single digits versus the prior year. Net interest margin is expected to rebound into the mid-3.60%s in the fourth quarter. David Turner said, "We now expect full-year 2025 net interest income to grow between 3% and 4%." Adjusted noninterest income is projected to grow between 4% and 5% versus 2024, and adjusted noninterest expense is expected to be up approximately 2% for the full year.

Financial Results

  • The quarter saw a strong performance in noninterest revenue, with wealth management generating another quarter of record fee income and capital markets, excluding CVA, reaching a record high. Adjusted noninterest income increased 6% linked quarter. Service charges rose 6%, and capital markets income, excluding CVA, increased 22% compared to the prior quarter. Adjusted noninterest expense increased 4% compared to the prior quarter, with salaries and benefits up 2%. Annualized net charge-offs as a percentage of average loans increased 8 basis points to 55 basis points. The allowance for credit losses decreased $30 million, and the ratio was reduced 2 basis points to 1.78%. The company ended the quarter with an estimated common equity Tier 1 ratio of 10.8%; adjusted to include AOCI, it increased from 9.3% to 9.5% quarter-over-quarter.

Q&A

  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous LLP: Asked whether the charge-offs and portfolio shaping were aligned with previously identified portfolios of interest and for an update on other portfolios being watched. John Turner responded that office and transportation portfolios have been under scrutiny for some time, with most charge-offs related to office. He highlighted improvements: "We had over $900 million in reductions, almost $1 billion in reductions in classified loans during the quarter. Some portion of that was a result of upgrades."
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Inquired about the rationale behind portfolio shaping actions. John Turner said it's driven by both credit risk appetite and returns, emphasizing, "we're aligning our credit risk appetite with expected returns in portfolios." David Turner added that the year's big change was in the leverage lending portfolio.
  • Steven Alexopoulos, TD Cowen: Sought clarity on margin guidance and deposit betas. David Turner explained, "The beauty of our hedging portfolio is to protect us as rates come down... we've been able to have a pretty resilient net interest margin regardless of what rate environment that we have."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked whether current portfolio reductions could continue to increase. John Turner stated, "We don't anticipate any significant additional reductions," but noted ongoing reviews and capital allocation discipline.
  • Dave Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about the possibility for loan growth to outpace GDP next year. David Turner reiterated expectation to grow in line with GDP plus a little, pending guidance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions reflected a neutral to slightly positive tone, with some focus on credit risk, loan growth prospects, and the sustainability of positive operating leverage.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, emphasizing strong credit quality, soundness of strategic plans, and capital allocation discipline. During Q&A, responses remained measured and consistent, with John Turner stating, "we feel really good about our NDFI exposure" and David Turner consistently referencing confidence in guidance and cost controls. Compared to the previous quarter, management sentiment remained steady, with a slightly greater emphasis on credit discipline and risk management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reported higher earnings and EPS compared to the previous quarter, with a continued strong return on tangible common equity. Guidance for net interest income growth narrowed slightly to between 3% and 4% from the prior 3%–5% range. Credit discipline and portfolio shaping remained a focus, with targeted loan exits and improvements in criticized loans. The tone of management and analysts remained similar, with management reiterating confidence in strategy and analysts maintaining a focus on loan growth, margin sustainability, and credit quality. Strategic priorities continue to emphasize technology modernization, risk-adjusted returns, and deposit growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing headwinds from higher-risk leveraged lending and continued elevated losses in certain portfolios through the fourth quarter. John Turner noted, "The only other area that we're seeing maybe a little elevated risk is in telecommunications." The company expects full-year net charge-offs to be approximately 50 basis points and has reserved for remaining anticipated losses. Analysts questioned whether further portfolio shaping could present future headwinds, to which management reiterated ongoing review but did not anticipate significant additional reductions.

Final Takeaway

Regions Financial Corporation emphasized strong earnings growth, resilient deposit and loan performance, and ongoing portfolio shaping focused on credit risk and returns. Management highlighted robust noninterest income, record results in wealth management and capital markets, and continued investment in technology modernization. The outlook for 2025 includes net interest income growth of 3%–4% and stable-to-modestly growing loan and deposit balances, with a consistent focus on prudent capital allocation and maintaining flexibility to adapt to evolving market and regulatory conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ferrellgas announces pricing of $650M senior notes offering
data.description:
• Ferrellgas (OTCPK:FGPR) and its wholly-owned subsidiary Ferrellgas Finance have priced an offering of $650M aggregate principal amount of 9.250% senior notes due 2031 at an offering price equal to 100% of the principal thereof.
  • The offering of the notes is expected to close on or about October 27, 2025.
  • The issuers intend to use the net proceeds received from the offering of the notes, together with cash on hand, to redeem all of their 5.375% senior notes due 2026. The redemption of the 2026 notes is conditioned upon the completion of the proposed offering of notes and the entry into an amendment to the credit agreement governing the Company’s existing revolving credit facility.
  • Source: Press Release
